title: Nudge Economics as Libertarian Paternalism

title: Ecodependence: The Intrinsic Connection between Living and Killing

title: Intersectionality, Decoloniality, Indigenous Localism: A Critique

title: Social Media and the Digital Structural Transformation of the Public Sphere
author: Staab, Thiel

opening_move.description: The paper opens by reconstructing Habermas's triad of mediality-subjectivity-accumulation in the structural transformation thesis, then diagnosing a gap: subsequent deliberative democratic theory lost this political-economic grounding, focusing instead on normative and institutional questions while neglecting how capitalism drives the digital transformation. The puzzle is: what does the digital structural transformation look like if we restore the political-economic analysis?
opening_move.stakes_claimed: The digital transformation represents a fourth stage of structural transformation (following bourgeois, industrial, and welfare-state-Fordist stages), characterized by 'privatisation without privatism' — commercial politicisation that threatens democratic will formation by replacing civic participation with preference extraction.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper performs immanent critique of Habermas — accepting his structural transformation framework while arguing that his later work abandoned its political-economic foundations. It then constructs a synthesis by mobilizing three theoretical frameworks along Habermas's original triad: Zuboff for mediality (data behaviourism), Reckwitz for subjectivity (singularisation), and Staab's own prior work on proprietary markets for accumulation. The resulting synthesis identifies a fourth stage of structural transformation.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Theoretical synthesis of secondary literature (Zuboff, Reckwitz, Staab) applied to an immanent reconstruction of Habermas's theory of structural transformation. Historical political economy of capitalism (Fordism, accumulation crises, post-Fordist individualisation).
epistemic_mode.notes: Despite the paper's concern with empirical phenomena (social media platforms, data collection, singularisation), all empirical claims are borrowed from secondary theoretical literature (Zuboff, Reckwitz, Staab's own prior work) rather than original research. The paper's contribution is entirely theoretical: the synthesis of Habermasian structural transformation theory with political economy of digital capitalism. Specific examples (Volkswagen production, Google/Facebook advertising) function as illustrations within a theoretical argument, not as independent empirical evidence. The paper explicitly acknowledges its narrow focus on social media as an 'experimental hypothesis' rather than an empirical claim. The epistemic mode is fully_normative — a conceptual reconstruction of a theoretical tradition.

normative_stake.domain: democratic theory / public sphere / digital capitalism

confidence_notes: The immanent_critique is true: the paper targets Habermas's own later work, arguing that his 'linguistic turn' toward discourse ethics and deliberative proceduralism led him to abandon the political-economic analysis that was the strength of the original structural transformation theory. The engagement_structure is friendly_amendment because the paper's goal is to restore and update Habermas's original approach rather than abandon it. The move_sequence is: genealogical_reconstruction (Habermas's three-stage theory) → immanent_critique (later Habermas and deliberative democracy lost political economy) → rival_mapping (two strands of social media research: platform political economy vs. communication dynamics — both partial) → framework_proposal (three-theorem synthesis: data behaviourism + singularisation + proprietary markets = fourth stage) → normative_derivation ('privatisation without privatism', 'representation without public sphere') → conclusion_open. This paper is structurally very similar to seeliger-sevignani-2022 (same special issue, Habermasian reconstruction, no case, fully normative) but makes a more original theoretical contribution through the three-theorem synthesis and the political-economic update. The normative stake is high because the paper concludes that 'data-driven elections' represent a fundamental threat to democratic will formation — the substitution of preference extraction for political participation.

title: Pastoral Power: Perspectives on the Present
author: Stypinska, Rossi

The paper argues these overlooked dimensions are precisely what makes pastoral power analytically valuable for understanding contemporary power formations.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Pastoral power continues to directly animate governmental techniques across neoliberal and neo-authoritarian contexts (digital platforms, self-optimization, debt subjectivity, ecological apocalyptics), and only by attending to its theological specificity — not just its secular governmental forms — can we understand these contemporary formations.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper performs immanent critique of Foucauldian governmentality studies — accepting the pastoral power concept while arguing that its appropriation by governmentality studies has erased its ethical, veridictional and theological specificity, reducing it to a secular administrative paradigm. The paper advocates restoring the theological dimension (truth-subjectivity triangulation) while situating it in productive dialogue with political and economic theology (Agamben, Stimilli, Esposito).

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: As a special section introduction, the paper reconstructs the Foucauldian pastoral power concept, maps its appropriation in governmentality studies, diagnoses a gap, outlines two problematiques (truth-subjectivity and theological subtexts), and previews the contributions of the other articles. The contemporary examples cited (algorithms, self-optimization discourse, digital influencing) function as symptoms that motivate the theoretical inquiry rather than as empirical evidence for specific claims. The paper's intellectual work is entirely conceptual: reconstructing the pastoral concept, critiquing its assimilation into governmentality studies, and framing the special section's two problematiques.

normative_stake.domain: political theology / subjectivity / governmentality

title: Apocalypse Forever? Post-political Populism and the Spectre of Climate Change
author: Swyngedouw

opening_move.description: The paper opens with a paradox: climate change is seemingly politicized as never before (on policy agendas, global consensus, mass media attention), yet the same period has seen the consolidation of a post-political and post-democratic condition (per Žižek, Rancière, Mouffe) in which genuine political contestation is evacuated from public life. The paper asks: how can these two apparently contradictory developments coexist? The resolution is the paper's core argument.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Hegemonic climate change discourse is not a form of politicization but a key mechanism through which a post-political, post-democratic configuration is forged and entrenched. Genuine democratic environmental politics requires reclaiming the space of dissensus and naming proper political subjects.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper synthesizes post-foundational political philosophy (Rancière, Žižek, Mouffe, Badiou) with political ecology and geography. It takes the post-political condition at face value as diagnosed by these thinkers and extends their framework to the environmental domain, arguing that climate change is a primary arena through which post-political depoliticization is accomplished. It draws on Laclau's theory of populism and Žižek's Lacanian analysis of empty signifiers and commodity fetishism to read CO2 as the 'objet petit a' of environmental politics.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Discourse analysis of climate change media coverage (headlines from The Independent, 2006), policy documents (Kyoto Protocol, UK Pathways to 2050 equivalent), political speeches (Prince Charles), corporate communications (BP rebranding), and Al Gore's film An Inconvenient Truth. Enumeration of CO2 derivatives market growth (zero to 463 million tons, 2005–2009) as illustration.
data_sources: European Climate Exchange CO2 futures data
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Lacanian-Žižekian analysis of ideological fantasy (apocalyptic imaginary, CO2 as objet petit a); Rancièrian analysis of the partition of the sensible and post-democratic governance; Laclauian analysis of populist empty signifiers; political economy of carbon commodification (Bumpus and Liverman).
epistemic_mode.notes: The empirical material (media headlines, market data, policy documents) is illustrative — it demonstrates the characteristics of the populist-post-political climate discourse rather than constituting independent evidence for the theoretical argument. The argument is primarily derived from the post-foundational political philosophy tradition. However, the CO2 derivatives market data and policy document analysis do specific work (demonstrating commodification and techno-managerial governance) that goes beyond pure illustration. Classified as normative_with_qualitative_empirical with illustrative evidence.

case_type.case_name: Hegemonic climate change discourse (Al Gore's An Inconvenient Truth, Kyoto Protocol, UK climate policy)

normative_stake.domain: democratic theory / environmental politics / post-politics

The seven-characteristic analysis of climate change populism (universal threat, empty people, externalized enemy, techno-managerial fix, anti-left alignment, hegemonic appropriation of demands, no proper name) constitutes a systematic analytical contribution beyond the framework. title: The Mystical Foundation of Democratic Society, Mythmaking and Truth in The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance (John Ford 1962)
author: Ungureanu

opening_move.description: The paper opens by positioning itself within the revival of interest in film as philosophical medium, then diagnosing the need for a 'middle way' between democratic progressivism (Habermas) and deconstruction (Derrida) on the question of the aporetic foundation of democratic authority. The film The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance is introduced as a resource for thinking through this impasse.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A 'politics of truth' — genuine connection between political life, personal life, and truth-telling — is both possible and necessary for democratic legitimacy. Myths and narratives built on lies (like Stoddard's legend) produce 'damaged life' and kleptocratic democracy. Ford's film is a cautionary parable about the consequences of decoupling democracy from truth.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper constructs a 'middle-way critical Enlightenment perspective' through immanent critique of both Habermas (whose democratic progressivism is too teleological and obfuscates the violence of foundation) and Derrida (whose hyperbolic deconstruction dissolves into endless aporia without affirmative content). It engages specifically with Pippin's reading of the film as anti-Enlightenment rationalism, arguing against this that Ford's film actually calls for a politics of truth. The film analysis both demonstrates the limitations of the rival positions and grounds the author's own synthesis.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Close cinematic reading of The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance (dir. John Ford, 1962): scene-by-scene analysis of the film's narrative arc, character symbolism (Stoddard, Doniphon, Hallie, Pompey, Scott/Peabody), visual composition (images of Doniphon's coffin with cactus rose), dialogue, and structural devices (flashback frame narrative). Direct engagement with film stills.
data_sources: The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance (dir. John Ford, 1962)
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Philosophical hermeneutics: reading the film as a parable of democratic theory, treating its narrative structure as an argument about the relationship between law, violence, myth and truth. Cross-reading of film interpretation with political philosophy (Habermas, Derrida, Pippin).
epistemic_mode.notes: This is the canonical normative_with_interpretive_case paper in the corpus. The film is not mere illustration of a pre-established philosophical argument — the close reading of Ford generates the specific insight about the 'politics of truth' that distinguishes Ungureanu's position from both Pippin and from standard Habermasian and Derridean readings. The immanent critique of Pippin's reading is the argumentative crux: Ungureanu argues Ford's film does NOT counsel replacing truth with myth (Pippin's reading), but rather demonstrates the personal and political damage of doing so. The film analysis is constitutive of this counter-reading.

case_type.case_name: The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance (dir. John Ford, 1962)

normative_stake.domain: democratic theory / political philosophy / foundations

confidence_notes: This is one of the most clearly typed papers in the corpus: a philosophical paper using a film as an interpretive case to adjudicate between rival positions in democratic theory. The reflexive_move is true because the conclusion explicitly acknowledges the film's own blind spots and mythologizing tendencies — Ford underplays collective democratic agency, relies on gendered individual heroism, and the 'ideal' figure of Doniphon is itself a myth. The counter_objection_response targets specifically Pippin's reading of the film as anti-Enlightenment: Ungureanu argues this misreads Ford by ignoring what the film shows about the consequences of the legend (the 'almost dead' Shinbone, Stoddard's melancholy, Pompey's continued exclusion). The meta_reflexive_qualification is the conclusion's discussion of Ford's own blind spots. The normative stake is high and prescriptive: the paper concludes that democratic politics requires genuine truth-telling, not myth-fabrication. title: Miyazaki Hayao's Animism and the Anthropocene
author: Yoneyama

opening_move.description: The paper opens from Amitav Ghosh's diagnosis of a 'crisis of imagination' blocking genuine response to the climate crisis. The puzzle: we need alternative stories and ontologies, but existing frameworks (classical animism, new animism) fail to provide accessible ones for modern global audiences. Miyazaki's animated films are proposed as filling this gap — offering a 'critical animism' formed from within modernity as a critique of modernity.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Miyazaki's 'critical animism' is not just cultural resource but a potential paradigm-disrupting philosophy for the social sciences in the Anthropocene, challenging the foundational tripod of anthropocentrism, secularism, and Eurocentrism that underlies both the social sciences and dominant Anthropocene discourse.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper distinguishes three positions: (1) classical animism (Tylor) — appropriates animism within Eurocentric evolutionary framework; (2) new animism (Bird-David, Harvey) — revalues animism but remains trapped in Eurocentric academic translation, confined to hunter-gatherer cases, absent from contemporary Asia; (3) critical animism (Miyazaki/Yoneyama) — animism formed within modernity as critique of modernity, non-dualistic, place-based, globally accessible through popular culture. The paper also positions critical animism against the scientism of dominant Anthropocene discourse. Braidotti's posthumanism is noted as partially resonant but not central.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Close reading of Miyazaki's animated films (Spirited Away, Nausicaä, Princess Mononoke, My Neighbour Totoro, Ponyo), manga volumes, published interviews and collected writings (Miyazaki 1996, 2008a, 2013). Historical contextualisation of Shinto nationalism vs. folk animism in Japan; biographical narrative of Miyazaki's animism emerging from Minamata context.
data_sources: Miyazaki's films: Spirited Away (2001), Nausicaä (1984), Princess Mononoke (1997), My Neighbour Totoro (1988), Ponyo (2008)
Miyazaki's published writings and interviews (Starting Point, Turning Point)
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Philosophical reading of animated film as articulation of an alternative ontology/epistemology; comparative conceptual analysis of animism types; genealogical account of critical animism's emergence from Minamata context; reading of specific film scenes and character dialogue as expressions of philosophical positions.
epistemic_mode.notes: The film/manga material is interpretive case, not empirical evidence in the social-scientific sense. Miyazaki's films are read as philosophical texts expressing a coherent ontology (critical animism) that the paper theorizes. This is normative_with_interpretive_case: the films are the lens through which the conceptual argument about paradigm alternatives for the social sciences in the Anthropocene is developed. The case is generative — the concept of 'critical animism' is built from the film analysis rather than being pre-established and merely illustrated.

case_type.case_name: Miyazaki Hayao's animated films (Studio Ghibli), especially Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind and Princess Mononoke
